steps.title: Fastest path: mark the figure first
steps.content: For coordinate-circle questions, read the center and radius from the equation or graph the circle as a check. For diagram facts, mark equal radii, diameters, tangents, and arcs before doing algebra.

steps.title: Read the circle center from the graph
steps.content:
Use the visible symmetry instead of setting up perpendicular-bisector algebra. The points $(12,10)$ and $(12,4)$ are vertically aligned and have midpoint:
$$\left(12,\frac{10+4}{2}\right)=(12,7)$$
The point $(9,7)$ is also on the circle, exactly $3$ units left of $(12,7)$, so the center is visibly $(12,7)$.

steps.title: Find the radius
steps.content: From center $(12,7)$ to $(12,10)$ is $3$ units. From center $(12,7)$ to $(12,4)$ is also $3$ units, and from center $(12,7)$ to $(9,7)$ is $3$ units. Therefore, the radius is $r=3$.

steps.title: Calculate the circumference
steps.content:
The circumference is:
$$C=2\pi r=2\pi(3)=6\pi$$
Since $C=k\pi$, $k=6$. That confirms B.
